acceptance_targets: [CH2_CONTINUITY_TIMING] Pass if a cold reader can reconstruct a coherent Day 2 schedule from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:3 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:245 that leaves Haruki dead roughly six hours before the Chapter 3 8:00 PM meeting, with no line still pinning the aftermath to 9:30 AM or promising an "afternoon" formal meeting that cannot fit that handoff. Fail if the chapter still compresses the entire sequence into the morning.

[CH2_EMBER_EXAMPLE_LINE] Pass if the revised note at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:213 still reads unmistakably like Ember and still rationalizes the knife delivery, but no longer opens with "I noticed..." and no longer frames the knives as "additional supplies" in a direct "today's delivery" construction. Fail if a reader familiar with the style bible can still recognize the calibration example in the revised line on first read.

[CH2_RESCUE_CHAIN_LEGIBILITY] Pass if a cold reader can state, from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:149 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:185 alone, who tried to get help, what specific barrier stopped help from reaching Haruki in time, and why Noor stayed with the wound instead of leaving. Fail if the sequence still relies only on a single unanswered shout and a vague lock sound.

[CH2_EXPLANATORY_SCAFFOLD_TIC] Pass if the cited lines no longer stack multiple near-identical "look/expression/quality/sound/blink of someone or a man who..." constructions across the chapter and at least two of the cited moments are carried by unglossed physical detail rather than explanatory diagnosis. Fail if the revised chapter still uses that same scaffold in three or more of the cited places.
[R001] Ch2 line 246 no longer names conference panels, review committees, or podiums.
[R013] Ch2 line 288 does not describe the stairwell's material or acoustic properties.
[R037] Ch2 contains at most 3 uses of 'institutional.'
[R042] Chapter 02 contains em-dashes on no more than 9 prose lines (≈8%).
[R078] Chapter 02 contains at most 6 uses of 'something.'

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can reconstruct a coherent Day 2 schedule from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:3 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:245 that leaves Haruki dead roughly six hours before the Chapter 3 8:00 PM meeting, with no line still pinning the aftermath to 9:30 AM or promising an "afternoon" formal meeting that cannot fit that handoff. Fail if the chapter still compresses the entire sequence into the morning.
findings.problem: The chapter anchors the dossier drop before dawn, promises Viktor's formal meeting "this afternoon," and still ends at 9:30 AM on Day 2. That chronology contradicts the boundary and continuity packs, which place Haruki's death roughly six hours before an 8:00 PM Chapter 3 meeting and closer to midday than early morning. This is a canonical timeline break, not just an imprecise clock.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reclock the chapter at the source rather than patching only the last sentence. Either move the dossier discovery later by revising the opening time markers at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:3 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:5, or expand the elapsed time explicitly through the meeting, server-room, and cafeteria beats keyed at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:77, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:195, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:245 so Haruki's death and the knife delivery plausibly land around early afternoon and hand off cleanly to the 8:00 PM Chapter 3 meeting.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised note at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:213 still reads unmistakably like Ember and still rationalizes the knife delivery, but no longer opens with "I noticed..." and no longer frames the knives as "additional supplies" in a direct "today's delivery" construction. Fail if a reader familiar with the style bible can still recognize the calibration example in the revised line on first read.
findings.problem: Ember's knife-delivery note is a near-direct lift of a calibration-only style-bible example: it repeats the same "noticed" plus "additional supplies" plus "today's delivery" structure on a chapter-defining beat. That makes the voice feel copied from reference material instead of freshly authored for this scene, which is an explicit dialogue-register violation.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the cafeteria terminal message centered on snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:213 and adjust snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:211 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:215 only as needed. Keep Ember's polite customer-service cadence and the implication that the knives are being framed as ordinary utility, but generate fresh wording instead of the calibration-line scaffold.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can state, from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:149 through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:185 alone, who tried to get help, what specific barrier stopped help from reaching Haruki in time, and why Noor stayed with the wound instead of leaving. Fail if the sequence still relies only on a single unanswered shout and a vague lock sound.
findings.problem: The bleed-out scene asks the reader to sit through eleven minutes in a compact building full of panicked coworkers, but the failed-rescue chain never becomes operationally legible on the page. Noor shouts once, Marcus freezes, a lock clicks somewhere, and the chapter leaves the reader to invent why no one arrives or why Noor cannot secure help any other way.
findings.rewrite_direction: In the server-room sequence beginning at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:149 and extending through snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:185, add one or two concrete attempted actions and one concrete obstacle: Noor testing the door, trying a terminal or intercom, realizing she cannot leave pressure safely, discovering the corridor has sealed, or another specific failure. The scene needs to show why rescue fails, not merely imply that it probably did.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the cited lines no longer stack multiple near-identical "look/expression/quality/sound/blink of someone or a man who..." constructions across the chapter and at least two of the cited moments are carried by unglossed physical detail rather than explanatory diagnosis. Fail if the revised chapter still uses that same scaffold in three or more of the cited places.
findings.problem: The narration repeatedly defaults to the same explanatory scaffold, turning bodies and expressions into formulations like the look, energy, quality, sound, blink, or expression of someone or a man who is then decoded for the reader. Used this often in one chapter, the construction stops feeling like Noor's sharp perception and starts feeling like autopilot prose.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the repeated explanatory sentences at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:43, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:65, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:99, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:123, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:157, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:161, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:171, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_02.md:187. Keep Noor's analytic intelligence, but vary sentence shape and let more of these beats land through raw action, sensory detail, or one cleaner inference instead of the recurring diagnostic template.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 line 246 no longer names conference panels, review committees, or podiums.
findings.evidence: Ch1 line 7: 'thirty years hunching over keyboards and podiums'; line 141: 'chairs committees for a living.' Ch2 line 246: 'the one built for moderating conference panels and chairing review committees.'
findings.problem: Viktor's height-as-podium and panel-chairing identity restated one chapter after establishment at Ch1 lines 7 and 141.
findings.rewrite_direction: At line 246, cut the parenthetical gloss about moderating conference panels and chairing review committees. Let Viktor's voice cut through the noise on its own authority.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 line 288 does not describe the stairwell's material or acoustic properties.
findings.evidence: Ch1 line 67: 'concrete walls, echoing, unheated — her footsteps bounce.' Ch2 line 288: 'The stairwell is concrete and cold, the institutional kind that turns every footstep into an echo.'
findings.problem: Adjacent-chapter re-description (Ch1→Ch2). Ch2 re-describes the stairwell as 'concrete and cold' with 'footsteps into an echo' — near-identical to Ch1 line 67. First described Ch1 line 67.
findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch2 line 288, cut the stairwell material/acoustic re-description. Enter through the action: Noor following Haruki, the sound of his footsteps ahead.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 contains at most 3 uses of 'institutional.'
findings.evidence: 'Institutional' appears 39 times total. Ch2 has 8 instances (highest). Target: keep 8-10 manuscript-wide.
findings.problem: Ch2 uses 'institutional' 8 times in 5,150 words. The word has become a tic rather than a descriptor. Target: reduce to 8-10 total manuscript-wide.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep 2-3 instances where 'institutional' does specific work. Replace or cut the rest — vary with concrete descriptors rather than the same abstract adjective.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 02 contains em-dashes on no more than 9 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 02 has 47.9% em-dash density (57 of 119 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~9 (≈8% of 119 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 02 contains at most 6 uses of 'something.'
findings.evidence: 'Something' count: 15 in 5150 words = 1 per 343 words. Manuscript-wide: 252 in 79,023 = 1 per 314 (threshold: 1 per 800).
findings.problem: Chapter 02 uses 'something' 15 times in 5150 words (1 per 343), contributing to the manuscript-wide overuse of 252 total. Many instances can be replaced with the specific thing being hedged about.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Chapter 02 to ~6 instances (≈1 per 800 words). Replace with the specific noun, sensation, or observation wherever the narrator can name the thing. Keep 'something' only where genuine uncertainty is the point.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
